data.title: Global X 2026 Outlook - 10 In EM: Back To Basics
data.description:
International Allocations

Emerging Markets (EM) appear poised to enter 2026 with the right mix of ingredients for a potentially positive cycle.

These include a weaker or steady U.S. dollar, increased liquidity in the financial system, strength in China, and low investor positioning in EM securities. These factors drove the last EM upswing, when the MSCI Emerging Markets Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index 344.4% to 15.07% (cumulative) between January 1, 2001, and December 31, 2010.[1] We believe those conditions could again be supportive for EM now. Against this backdrop, we aim to balance cyclical and structural growth stories into the new year with a focus on valuations and profitability versus cost of capital. Our highest-conviction ideas take investors back to BASICs, as we see potential opportunities in Brazil, Argentina, Southeast Asia, India, and China (specifically within consumer), among other themes and regions.

Key EM Opportunities

What's Moving the Needle?

U.S. Dollar

The dollar fell roughly 8% in the first 10 months of 2025, as measured by the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY).[2] A dovish Federal Reserve, widening growth differentials favoring international markets, and public acknowledgment from Washington that a weaker dollar supports trade all added downward pressure.

Liquidity

The Fed’s renewed easing cycle has injected liquidity back into the system. Over the past 25 years, EM equities have outperformed the S&P 500 Index by an average of 11.9% and 15.4% during the 6- and 12-month periods following the final cut of Fed rate easing cycles.[3] EM dollar-denominated bonds have also exceeded U.S. Investment-Grade bonds by 4.6% and 8.8% in those same windows.[4]

China

The World Bank has upgraded its Chinese GDP growth expectations from 4% to 4.8%.[5] The October meeting between Presidents Trump and Xi marked an important step towards resolving bilateral tensions and fostering economic progress.

Positioning

From a positioning perspective, most advisors we speak to still have, on average, less than 5% exposure to EM equities. However, 2025 was the first time in many years that we heard noticeable concerns about underweights to the asset class and a desire to get closer to the 10.5% benchmark weight in the MSCI All Country World Index.

Icing On The Cake?

In addition to these high-level macroeconomic drivers, we acknowledge that politics can also play a key role in EM investing. In Latin America, the 2025-2026 period brings various elections, and we have already seen the political pendulum begin to swing from the extreme left back to the center-right. India should continue to thrive under Prime Minister Modi and reforms initiated by the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP). Greece’s New Democracy Party continues to focus on both debt reduction and growth. Even the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) has stepped in the direction of neighboring Japan and Korea in terms of market-oriented reforms.

With these factors in place, we believe 2025 could be the first inning of a multi-year uptrend for Emerging Market assets.

Despite our bullish outlook, we acknowledge that broad EM index exposure isn’t for everyone and highlight some regional and country-specific options to gain exposure to the growth opportunities and attractive valuations we see across Emerging Markets.

1 - Argentina - High Flying, Adored

Monitoring

Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) Acceleration

Commodity and infrastructure projects should attract new foreign investment.

Foreign Exchange (FX) Flexibility

Authorities are likely to continue easing currency controls, eventually reaching a free float.

More Tax & Labor Reforms

Congress may fast-track a reform package simplifying the tax code and modernizing labor rules to boost formal employment.

Pension & Fiscal Consolidation

Targeted spending cuts should anchor a medium-term fiscal adjustment, reinforcing International Monetary Fund (IMF) support.

MSCI Index Inclusion

As liquidity improves and foreign access expands, Argentina could requalify for MSCI Emerging Markets status, potentially unlocking a new wave of institutional flows.

We believe Argentina’s equity market now offers one of the most asymmetric opportunities in Emerging Markets. Credible policy, cheap valuations, and rising institutional engagement position it for a potential multi-year rerating.

President Javier Milei’s October 2025 decisive midterm victory reinforced investor confidence and expanded his ability to deliver market-friendly structural reforms. Equities recently traded at roughly 8.5x forward earnings, well below regional peers, potentially leaving room for multiple expansion as fundamentals strengthen.[6] We see parallels to Brazil in 1995: inflation is cooling, reforms are gaining traction, and markets are awakening after years of dysfunction.

Milei has done much of the “heavy lifting” in his first two years in office, and his efforts have delivered results (higher growth, lower inflation, lower poverty).

However, there are plenty of opportunities for improvement.

2 - Colombia - The Next Argentina?

Monitoring

Congressional Elections

March 8, 2026.

Presidential Elections

May 31st and June 21st, 2026, for a run-off.

Energy Prices

Oil and oil derivatives represent Colombia’s number one export.[9]

Venezuela

Potential political change.

Staying in Latin America with a focus on political change, Colombian equities may present one of the most compelling contrarian opportunities in the region. Valuations remain deeply discounted, reflecting years of policy uncertainty and perceived mismanagement, yet the upcoming May Presidential election could mark a turning point. A market-friendly political shift could unlock investment, stabilize fiscal policy, and reignite growth. Moreover, Colombia stands to benefit indirectly from potential regime change in Venezuela, with improved trade, migration normalization, and regional sentiment offering tailwinds. In our view, Colombia is priced for stagnation but positioned for recovery, creating an attractive setup for investors seeking potential asymmetric upside.

3 - Brazil - Two Catalysts Align

Monitoring

The Central Bank

We expect the COPOM to begin cutting rates in Q1 of 2026.

Tarcisio de Freitas

The Governor of Sao Paulo needs to announce if he is running for president by April of 2026.

Presidential Elections

First round on October 4, 2026. Run-off would be on October 25th.

Brazil goes into 2026 with two key performance drivers: interest rates and politics. In terms of monetary policy, Brazil’s key rate is poised to end 2025 at 15%. With inflation around 5%, that translates into one of the highest real rates in the world, which should support a powerful carry trade and buoy the Brazilian real (BRL).[10] We expect rate cuts beginning in the first quarter of 2026, which would likely act as a powerful standalone force for Brazilian equities. The prevalence of floating-rate debt in Brazil means lower interest rates should reduce net interest expenses for corporations and lift earnings (before we see other positive effects in the form of improved asset quality, more loan growth, more capex, and growth!). Looking at the past seven completed rate cutting cycles in Brazil, the average MSCI Brazil Index (Net) rally stood at roughly +95% (cumulative), where the average drawdown was around -15% (cumulative).[11] We like this risk-reward set-up.

Looking further out, Brazil hosts Presidential elections in October of 2026. The incumbent is expected to run for his fourth term in office and, if elected, would be 81 by the start of his new term. He has recently faced multiple health challenges and a high rejection rate. Noticing a pattern of Senate and Congressional elections moving towards the center-right, we believe that there is a legitimate opportunity for a market-friendly, fiscally responsible center-right candidate to step into the presidency.

4 - India - A Standalone Growth Opportunity

Monitoring

Trade

We expect continued progress on trade talks with the U.S. along with implementation of the new free trade agreement with the EU.

Consumption

We expect an uptick in consumer data as the government’s recent stimulus filters through the economy.

Foreign Direct Investment

We expect the “Make in India” program to continue driving multinational investment into India.

India lagged the rest of EM through the first 10 months of 2025 due to trade uncertainty, but we believe that various near-term factors could support equity market performance. On the policy front, a dovish Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and recent consumer-friendly fiscal reforms could lend support. Commodities are another potential tailwind in the form of low energy prices (India imports ~90% of its energy needs) and high gold prices (~20% of household savings lie in gold).[12, 13] Any profit taking from China’s recent rally could also be a catalyst, as could steps towards a U.S./India trade deal. Longer term, we see India offering compelling long-term growth potential and serving as an enticing standalone investment.

5 - Southeast Asia - Diversified Value and Growth

ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nation) equities offer a compelling blend of diversification and structural growth potential, supported by favorable demographics and policy stability. The region stands out as a key beneficiary of global supply chain diversification away from China, with Indonesia and Malaysia expanding semiconductor capacity and Vietnam, Malaysia, and Thailand emerging as alternative sources for rare earth elements. This shift is attracting record FDI inflows and deepening ASEAN’s role in global manufacturing. Combined with diverse economic drivers, from energy in Malaysia to tourism in the Philippines and Thailand, the bloc provides investors with multi-sector exposure to one of the world’s most compelling long-term growth stories.

Monitoring

“Friendshoring”

We expect more Western companies to diversify supply chains out of China and across SE Asia.

Rare Earths

We are keeping an eye on potential policy shifts and trade agreements in the critical mineral space.

Semiconductors

We are monitoring government incentives and foreign direct investment into semiconductor manufacturing.

6 - Vietnam - Good Morning, Vietnam!

Monitoring

Index Inclusion

Following the announcement from FTSE, we are monitoring language for potential inclusion of Vietnam into the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

"China +1"

We expect capacity expansion in electronics, semiconductors, and other high value manufacturing segments as both Chinese and Western companies diversify production into the country.

Reforms

We look for the continuation of deregulation and the government’s shrinking role in the economy.

We think Vietnam is entering 2026 positioned to accelerate. The nation is benefitting from global supply chain diversification and the “China+1” narrative, contributing to the government’s target of 10% (!) annual GDP growth in 2026.[14] Finally, the recent FTSE Russell upgrade from Frontier to Emerging Market status unlocks billions in passive flows, and we believe an MSCI upgrade should follow.

7 - China - Assessing the Positives and Negatives

China is a large, complex, and dynamic economy. We unpack our key areas of focus for 2026 and their potential implications on markets:

POSITIVE NEGATIVE

Trade

Recent progress on trade negotiations should boost equity market sentiment. However, exports now contribute only ~2% to GDP growth.[15] China has also diversified its demand base. Less than 15% of exports went to the U.S. in 2024, down from 21% in 2002.[16] The real priority for Beijing is easing U.S. tech export controls, not boosting goods trade.

Taiwan

Tensions persist, but Beijing’s strategic calculus remains pragmatic: it seeks stability ahead of any U.S. policy shifts under Trump, avoiding conflict that could disrupt vital imports like semiconductors, LNG, and iron ore.

Technology

President Xi’s agenda centers on technological self-reliance. R&D spending has surged 251% since 2012, with breakthroughs in robotics, EVs, and green tech.[17] Beijing aims to reduce dependence on Western inputs while leveraging its near-monopoly in rare earth processing as strategic leverage in future negotiations.[18]

Demographics

With a shrinking labor force, China is countering demographic decline through automation and education investment. Over half of global industrial robots are now installed in China, and domestic firms lead production, elevating productivity even as the population ages. China’s robot density ranked third globally in 2023, at 470 units per 10,000 manufacturing employees, well ahead of the U.S. (295).[20]

Rare Earths

“The Middle East has oil. China has rare earths.” Deng Xiaoping, 1992. China dominates global refining of critical minerals and produces 92% of global rare earth magnets, which are essential components in many U.S. weapons, among other key goods.[19]

Capital Markets

China is showing early signs of pursuing governance reforms and shareholder-friendly initiatives similar to the “value-up” programs seen in Korea and Japan. Regulators are encouraging higher dividend payouts, buybacks, and improved transparency, potentially unlocking value in a deeply discounted equity market trading well below historical averages.

Consumer!

The consumer discretionary sector aligns with Beijing’s long-term policy objectives around “common prosperity” and doubling the middle class by 2035. Meanwhile, China’s pivot toward value-added technology supports innovation-driven growth. The sector spans tech, internet, autos, travel, hospitality, and education, offering investors diversified exposure to the rising purchasing power and policy-backed transformation of the Chinese economy.

Monitoring

Technology

China’s focus on self-sufficiency in semiconductors, AI, and green tech will likely intensify in 2026 as it seeks to offset export controls and sustain innovation-led growth.

Consumption Stimulus

The CCP continues to emphasize growing domestic consumption, but we are yet to see the necessary stimulus or social safety nets to move the needle.

Geopolitics

Potential reemergence of U.S.–China tensions and supply-chain realignments will keep external risks elevated.

8 - Greece - Greece is Good

Monitoring

Index inclusion

Following the announcement from FTSE, we are watching language for potential inclusion of Greece into the MSCI Developed Markets Index.

M&A

We are monitoring headlines around potential targets in the Greek financial space from Western European peers.

Greek equities have shown remarkable momentum of late, with the MSCI Greece Index (Net) climbing over 70% year-to-date through October 31, 2025.[21] Sovereign upgrades to Investment-Grade status by all three major rating agencies came from a result of responsible governance, fiscal discipline, and EU backing. Similarly, FTSE has already announced the upgrade of Greece to Developed Market status. MSCI could be next, which would likely provide a catalyst by unlocking passive inflows. Aided by Recovery and Resilience Facility (RRF) fund inflows and tourism, Greek GDP is projected to expand 2.2% in 2026, ahead of Eurozone peers.[22] Despite all of these positives and strong fundamentals, the MSCI Greece Index recently traded at a price-to-earnings ratio of 8.7x and a price-to-book ratio of 1.2x, well below the S&P 500 Index.[23] Overall, we believe that Greek equities offer EM-style growth and valuations with DM-level stability, yet they are still underowned and poised for a potential re-rating.

9 - EM Bonds - Potential Yield and Diversification Benefits

Emerging Market fixed income enters 2026 on strong footing. Disinflation, resilient growth, and Fed easing support potential flows and performance. In addition to yield and diversification benefits, we also see opportunities for growth as we note improved fiscal discipline in key countries. Overall, we believe EM debt can provide compelling risk-adjusted income as global rates normalize and risk appetite improves.

Monitoring

Monetary Policy

EM dollar-denominated bonds have historically rallied and outperformed U.S. Investment-Grade bonds in the six- and twelve-month periods following the final Fed rate cut.

The USD

Weakness in the U.S. dollar should support EM dollar-denominated bond performance as it lowers external debt burdens and boosts commodity-linked revenues supporting credit conditions.

Positioning

EM debt is often underrepresented in both global bond indices and institutional portfolios.

10 - Commodities - Building Blocks of EM Performance

Commodities and Emerging Markets have long moved in lockstep, not only because EM countries tend to be both the largest producers and consumers of raw materials, but because they are both subject to fluctuations in the U.S. dollar. Looking into the start of 2026, we expect to see improving global GDP growth, especially in more cyclical sectors, to drive more economically sensitive commodities higher while we believe we remain in a structural bull market in precious metals. We also continue to see medium-term supply deficits building in key areas such as copper, lithium, and energy.

Monitoring

Precious Metals

Gold stands to benefit from a weaker USD and continued central bank buying, while rising gold prices act as a powerful tailwind for EM consumer balance sheets, especially those in India and China. Higher Gold prices should benefit producers, as well. Silver carries similar traits to gold but should prove more economically sensitive to cyclical recoveries.

Copper

Copper would likely be a key beneficiary of any cyclical recovery, while robust structural demand (electrification, urbanization, and artificial intelligence) and a challenged supply outlook (declining grades, aging mines, and rising jurisdictional risk) should support prices throughout the cycle.

Energy

Oil prices remain subject to changes in OPEC+ supply quotas in the short term, while the nuclear power revival and challenged supply support Uranium market fundamentals.

In Conclusion

In finding a broad and balanced allocation, we like the idea of balancing value and growth along with economies that benefit from both cyclical and structural trends.

B

BRAZIL

High real rates create an attractive carry trade and margin expansion setup. The next rate-cutting cycle could ignite a broader equity re-rating as politics trend more market-friendly ahead of the 2026 election.
A

ARGENTINA

Reform momentum, credible policy execution, and attractive valuations point to one of EM’s most asymmetric opportunities as FX flexibility, FDI inflows, and potential MSCI re-inclusion drive re-engagement.
S

SOUTHEAST ASIA

The region plays a significant role in global supply chain diversification, with rising FDI in semiconductors, energy, and logistics supporting ongoing growth and policy stability.
I

INDIA

A dovish RBI, consumer-focused fiscal policy, and low energy prices support near-term upside, while structural reforms and digital innovation support long-term compounding.
C

CHINA CONSUMER

Post–Trump/Xi talks, trade friction risk eases as Beijing prioritizes tech self-reliance, automation, and governance reforms, laying the groundwork for potential flows to consumer and innovation leaders.

For example, legal, political, and economic structures in these countries may be changing rapidly, which can cause instability and greater risk of loss. These countries are also more likely to experience higher levels of inflation, deflation, or currency devaluation, which could hurt their economies and securities markets. For these and other reasons, investments in emerging markets are often considered speculative. Similarly, investors are also subject to foreign securities risks including, but not limited to, the fact that foreign investments may be subject to different and in some circumstances less stringent regulatory and disclosure standards than U.S. investments.

data.title: Touchstone Mid Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Utilizes a classic value-driven fundamental investment process
  • Seeks to identify companies believed to be selling at a discount to their intrinsic value
  • Employs five valuation screens that seek to identify attractively priced securities
  • Conducts in-depth research and analysis on the securities that pass the valuation screens in an effort to identify leading companies selling at attractive valuations
  • Examines financial statements and assesses the company’s management team, competitive strategy and its current market position

Market Recap

Style factors across our asset class were a headwind to performance. Within the Russell Midcap® Value Index, high volatility stocks notably outperformed more defensive, low volatility positions. There was little correlation across price-to-earnings valuations, though non-earners performed best. Stocks with no yield outperformed those with high dividend yields. Stocks in the highest quintiles of return on equity outperformed those in the lower quintiles.

Within the benchmark, the best performing sectors were Information Technology (IT), Consumer Discretionary, and Utilities. Consumer Staples, Real Estate, Energy, Materials, and Health Care sectors notably underperformed.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Mid Cap Value Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived)(TCVAX) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell Midcap® Value Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.

The Fund’s sector level relative performance was mixed. Consumer Discretionary, Financials, Energy, and Industrials were the largest sector laggards. Relative performance was strongest in Health Care, Utilities, and IT.

The Utilities sector was a modest contributor to performance led by Xcel Energy Inc. (XEL). The regulated utility has been trading at a discount to peers due to questions about liability in the case of a Colorado fire. Late in the quarter, prior to the start of a trial, the company reached a settlement for an amount much smaller than anticipated by the market.

The IT sector contributed to relative performance, led by strong results from Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) and PTC Inc. (PTC). LITE reported strong results and raised 2025 guidance expectations as telecom and datacenter growth for artificial intelligence (AI) and cloud computing continue to boost demand for the company’s optical solutions. Software company PTC reported a strong pipeline of new business, a benefit stemming from a change in their sales organization, and the company was also the subject of takeover speculation. EPAM Systems Inc. (EPAM) was a notable underperformer in the sector. The software engineering firm reported solid results but lagged in a market that was narrowly focused on AI.

The Materials sector was a modest detractor during the quarter, with Ball Corp. (BALL), the worst performing name. The aluminum packaging company declined as beer consumption was weaker than expected, though increased energy and soft drink demand should be enough to offset any earnings weakness. However, Eagle Materials Inc. (EXP) outperformed as pricing for cement and wallboard held up better than expected despite choppy demand patterns for both materials.

Darling Ingredients Inc. (DAR) was the largest detractor in the Consumer Staples sector. The rendering and biofuels company continues to wait for final rules from the Environmental Protection Agency regarding renewable volume obligations and small refinery exemptions, and capping prices for their products over the short term.

The Industrials sector weighed on relative returns in the quarter, despite solid results from several holdings. Industrial distributor WESCO International Inc. (WCC) outperformed given their favorable exposure to data center builds. Two defense companies also outperformed, Leidos Holdings Inc. (LDOS) and L3Harris Technologies Inc. (LHX). Leidos’ multiple has recovered following solid results and the end of DOGE-related fears from earlier in the year. LHX has product offerings that align well with the priorities of the new administration: golden dome, space, and munitions restocking.

The Financials sector underperformed during the quarter. Pinnacle Financial Partners Inc. (PNFP), a bank with a strong franchise in the southeastern United States, announced an unexpected merger with Synovus Financial Corp. (SNV). We expect share losses and a difficult integration, and we exited the position as a result. Reinsurance Group of America Inc. (RGA) lagged in the quarter, as a reversal of positive mortality rates resulted in an earnings miss. Ameriprise Financial Inc. (AMP) experienced headwinds from low customer cash balances, reduced asset flows in their wrap platform, and increased competition for advisor acquisitions. On the positive side, investment bank Evercore Inc. (EVR) benefited from increased advisory fees and tight expense control.

The largest underperforming sector in the Fund was the Consumer Discretionary sector, led by LKQ Corp. (LKQ), an auto salvage and parts company. The company continues to see weak repair volumes as customers have been reluctant to fix damaged vehicles due to rising auto insurance rates. Auto parts manufacturers Gentex Corp. (GNTX) and BorgWarner Inc. (BWA) both outperformed in the quarter on good execution and more clarity around potential tariff related headwinds.

As we enter the fourth quarter, we are notably underweight in the Real Estate and Communication Services sectors. These exposures are driven by the opportunity set we see in each sector. During the quarter we initiated positions in Microchip Technology (MCHP) (IT sector), SouthState Bank Corp. (SSB) (Financials sector), Packaging Corp. (PKG)(Materials sector), and Schlumberger Limited (SLB)(Energy sector). We exited our positions in ChampionX Corp. (Energy sector), Columbia Sportswear Co. (COLM)(Consumer Discretionary sector), NiSource Inc. (NI)(Utility sector), Pinnacle Financial Partners Inc. (Financials sector), and Qorvo Inc. (QRVO)(IT sector).

Outlook and Conclusion

Crosswinds persist as we move into the final quarter of 2025. While a well-telegraphed reduction in interest rates boosted returns in the third quarter, the pace of future rate reductions is still in question, as is the independence of the U.S. Federal Reserve. Inflation continues to run at elevated rates while job creation has stagnated, creating diametrically opposing signals for decision makers. Tariffs, policy changes, and a government shutdown are all creating unevenness across the global economy. The AI boom has been a key driver for markets over the last two years, and a valuation bubble continues to be actively debated among investors.

Amid these market dynamics, we continue to hold fast and invest according to our process. Fundamentally we are looking for quality stocks, trading at a discount, with good risk/reward. We look for companies with strong management teams, high barriers to entry, solid balance sheets, and we continue to rigorously examine downside scenarios for our positions.

We continue to find attractively valued investment opportunities with favorable risk/reward profiles. While we do not believe in making short-term projections, we believe these investments will outperform the market longer term.

data.title: Qualcomm Is Still Cheap As Its AI Story Gets Real
data.description:
QUALCOMM Bulls Are Buying The Dips

It's really not a bad time for the tech folks, who have been scooping up the stock of QUALCOMM (QCOM) since the November bottom. Investors keenly following the market tape are arguably well aware that tech stocks have come under more intense scrutiny, as the media narrative is harping on rotation to other cyclical and value plays. The battering that Oracle (ORCL) investors were handed this week has also worsened sentiments, while Broadcom's (AVGO) solid execution and conversion from its multiyear backlog clarity weren't enough to stem a post-earnings slide following its earnings release.

Questions are starting to cascade down from investors who were already suffering from the collapse in high risk/high reward speculative neocloud setups like CoreWeave (CRWV) that are still struggling for traction. The specter of the AI bubble implosion now appears more profound than before, as the purveyors of the AI doom begin fanning the flames for the biggest bust up since the throes of the dot com bear market.

Hence, it does seem like an unlikely corollary, despite being classified as a tech sector and semiconductor setup. After all, we do have to consider that QCOM has outperformed the S&P 500 since my last update in late September. Back then, I upgraded the QCOM, as I've started to see the firm's strategy to expand its opportunities beyond its mobile chipset business gaining a more solid foothold.

Market sentiments were also gleaned to be increasingly constructive, underpinning my assessment. Following that update, we all know that QUALCOMM unveiled its 200MW partnership with Saudi-based Humain that charts a pivotal foray into AI data centers. That development triggered a buying frenzy that saw the stock push beyond the $200 level for a moment, although the subsequent profit taking was also equally fast and furious. Despite that, QCOM's relative outperformance over the S&P 500 is still noteworthy, even after taking into account the slide that saw the market digest the surge.

QUALCOMM's Handsets Business Shows Resilience

Suffice to say, I think that QUALCOMM's more resilient and less worrisome core business in mobile chipsets, licensing, and also design pipeline into the long-term automotive growth vertical has helped to assure the market of its key revenue drivers.

Hence, what appears to be a material weakness in QUALCOMM's growth optionality could now be viewed as a strength, as we have also observed in Apple (AAPL) stock's most recent forays to take out new all-time highs through early December. The smartphone upgrade cycle has arguably improved market sentiments, while QUALCOMM's chip design prowess in the Android ecosystem continues to shine.

It's notable that QUALCOMM scored a 10% increase in handset revenue ex-Apple, showing that the stress sensors from the potential Apple exit in 2027 appear to be less onerous than feared. QUALCOMM CEO Cristiano Amon afforded us a timely reminder that the company's opportunities of going upmarket (into more premium tiers) have continued to gain momentum. Isn't that the strategy that has paid dividends for Apple's iOS ecosystem, which QUALCOMM is clearly emulating to a certain degree of success with its recent performance? In fact, when viewed over the past two years, QUALCOMM's handset revenue for the September quarter is up >11% against the same period two years ago (based on a trailing-12-month basis).

Therefore, it seems like the market is arguably seeking solace from QUALCOMM's more predictable resilience observed from its mobile chipsets revenue base. Note that it still accounts for >72% of its QCT segment (based on trailing 12 months), providing a firm anchor to grow QUALCOMM's faster-growing automotive/IoT pipeline into a $22B behemoth through FY2029.

Although the revenue conversion is still relatively small as compared to the burgeoning opportunity, automotive revenue has grown to nearly $4B in the recent quarter that printed just $1.5B in September 2023 (trailing 12 months basis). I believe these are crucial metrics showing that when given the opportunity to prove its mettle, what QUALCOMM really needs is the time and space for it to execute and deliver the results.

QUALCOMM's AI Data Center Foray Provides A Timely Boost

Which is why it unfolds nicely as we segue our discussion to QUALCOMM's timely entry into the AI data center prospects. Finding the right approach to compete head-on with Nvidia (NVDA) cannot be further overstated. After all, AMD (AMD) is already in the fray, while Broadcom has commanded a sizable lead in custom AI accelerators. It would have been highly challenging for QUALCOMM to wrestle material market share against these formidable peers, unless the company had a viable and credible strategy to convince the market.

Management thinks that its key opportunity lies in staking a claim in the AI inferencing market, beginning with the AI 200 and AI 250 chips. I think the key message is that QUALCOMM knows how to deliver AI on the edge, and if AI is expected to cascade to edge devices in the future, QUALCOMM should be years ahead of its archrivals in thinking about solving this quandary for its data center customers. The challenges have often revolved around trying to achieve low power, but highly efficient AI chips. Google's (GOOGL) (GOOG) capabilities with its TPUs (now in the seventh generation) show its understated prowess of having highly efficient chips not just for inference but also for training.

However, QUALCOMM's competitive opening isn't gunning for the "holy grail" in training workloads, but for a firm foothold in AI inferencing, leveraging its know-how in NPU architecture on compute and memory. The key narrative behind the company's strategy is focusing on the future opportunity of the "disaggregation of the data center," as Amon clearly presented:

The good thing about QUALCOMM, we don't have to get a lot (of market share). I think we only need to get a small portion of this very large tenant is very significant for the company. We also like the fact that a lot of the market is concentrated. You have a few customers that buy at scale. The different thing is the market welcomes competition. QUALCOMM is not a small semiconductor company. We can do things at scale. I think we have like a proven track record of executing in a number of different industry at scale. And I think the market has been very intrigued about what we're doing. We're doing something different. We're thinking about the next disaggregation of the data center. And we're excited about it. We will unveil details of the road map, both the AI 200, the AI 250, what we're going to be doing after the AI 250. - Cristiano Amon presents at UBS Global Technology and AI Conference 2025

Wall Street Wants More From QUALCOMM To Upgrade Estimates

Of course, Wall Street wants a lot more clarity into the roadmap, which the company has indicated it "will unveil." Until then, I believe the market will stay reticent on QCOM's revenue outlook in the near term, as seen above. Investors will likely still index QUALCOMM to its core competencies in mobile chipsets, while waiting for salient and material updates on a potential revenue growth inflection as it threads the needle with its AI data center prospects.

The intricacies of QUALCOMM's hyperscaler discussions will need to convince the market that QCOM isn't just trying to poke small holes at the top AI chip designers, but has managed to construct forward-looking negotiations with these big-spending hyperscalers that could clearly move the needle beyond what it has mustered with Humain.

Buy Or Sell QCOM Stock?

That said, QCOM isn't trading at an exorbitant forward earnings multiple right now. At a metric of just 15x, it's way below the Magnificent Seven's 30x multiple, while also coming below the S&P 500's >22x multiple to begin with. And if we even contemplate the semiconductor peer median of 32x (based on S&P Cap IQ data), it's pretty clear to me that the market hasn't quite bought into QCOM's AI data center optionality just yet.

That level of pessimism also offers an opening for high-conviction investors who have seen its annualized automotive revenue reach the $4B mark, berating the doubters who thought QCOM couldn't make it work. And if QUALCOMM manages to solve the low-power and expensive HBM memory puzzle (since QUALCOMM's approach doesn't rely on HBM for now), it's more than just a plausibility for QCOM to expand its clout beyond the Saudi-based Humain through the rest of the decade.

More details will definitely be necessary, but it also goes to show that the market has sized up QCOM's opportunity favorably, given its recent outperformance, while accounting for its relatively appealing valuation.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: AMD Enters A New Phase
data.description:
Reading Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.'s (AMD) recent presentations at the Barclays Global Technology Conference and Analyst and Investor Day, it’s evident that AMD is no longer talking about a turnaround.

The messaging has clearly shifted from proving relevance to scaling. There isn’t much discussion about short-term outcomes and very little discussion about stock price. The previous focus was on AI Inference and now it’s all about timescales for deployments, infrastructure limitations, and AMD’s role within the next decade of computing.

That shift in tone isn’t window dressing. It represents a shift in perspective for the company, from being a challenger expecting an uptick on a cyclical basis to being a core player in the enabling technologies for AI. The notion that AI represents an investment cycle spanning multiple decades, as opposed to a delicate outlay of spending, was consistent throughout both sessions.

Notably, executives conveyed that customers aren’t debating an expenditure question but an implementation tempo. That completely changes the conversation on all worries about AI capex.

The Infrastructure Bottleneck Is the Signal

The most reassuring message that came out of both forums was that it is not demand uncertainty that constrains growth. It’s clear that customers are funded, committed, and increasingly confident about unit economics. It’s not these aspects that constrain growth but constraints imposed by physical reality, factors such as power and data center readiness, advanced packaging capabilities, and memory supply.

By pointing at deployment constraints instead of softening demand, what executives are implicitly saying is that demand visibility and backlog are not an issue. The executives at AMD were very clear about the fact that if customers had more compute resources available today, they would put them all to immediate use.

That puts AMD on a completely different trajectory compared with businesses that rely on discretionary IT budgets. The spending on AI infrastructure will no longer be treated as discretionary capex but will be considered strategic.

The Silicon-Only Discipline Defines the Model

Perhaps the most telling takeaway from the Barclays discussion, nonetheless, regards AMD’s commitment to staying economically centered on silicon even as it begins extending itself into more comprehensive rack-scale solutions. Helios, MI450, and reference architectures are enablers and tools for customers and do not constitute a shift into the resale of systems. Selling Racks, servers, and commodity components will still be with OEM and ODM partners.

Its importance cannot be overstated. It preserves AMD’s margin foundation and rate structure. Again, it was reiterated that 55-58% still remains as the gross margin target because it doesn’t include low-value pass-through revenue. Helios will bring about a reduction in time-to-value, risk associated with integration, and simplified deployments. However, monetization will remain within GPUs, CPUs, and scale-up networking.

Within a market sector where some competitors are expanding upwards towards comprehensive systems, it may appear that AMD’s strategy is conservative. It merely represents an understanding of where value actually resides.

OpenAI Is a Catalyst, Not a Dependency

The OpenAI deal is considered a single big deal. It was more nuanced and, bluntly, more plausible as a single deal as described by management. OpenAI (OPENAI) is viewed more as an accelerator within an ecosystem and less as a risk of concentration. The value goes beyond and includes the six-gigawatt commitment and extends into how OpenAI uses its infrastructure with multiple hyperscalers and neoclouds.

All of this creates a very strong second-order effect. As a result of seeing AMD’s infrastructure proven on OpenAI’s platforms, it becomes easier for these customers and others to embrace something similar. The sales cycles shrink, and risks associated with platform integrations no longer exist because AMD’s platform will start appearing as a “default” solution instead of an alternative. It’s exactly what happened with EPYC.

It was also made clear that OpenAI is not alone in scale signaling. It is among several multi-gigawatt projects that are under discussion. The interpretation here is that AMD is developing a portfolio of long-term contracts as opposed to offering a quick solution.

ASICs, GPUs, and the Power of Flexibility

A question that kept popping up at both forums was: Will custom silicon disrupt the market for general-purpose GPUs? AMD's response: ASICs will exist, they will be successful at some things, and they will constitute 20-25% of the long-term acceleration market. But the rest needs programmability.

The debate is less about ideas and more about needs. Agentic workflows, inference, fine-tuning, and training are still developing at a rate that makes it hard for fixed-function silicon to play an ultimate role. The requirements stretch from developer needs and desires for more flexible solutions to customer needs and desires for more options. It is encouraging to note that AMD’s plan serves these needs.

It is at this juncture that AMD’s diversification of platforms assumes its importance. CPU, GPU, adaptive computing, and networking are being created as an interlocking puzzle with no product as an end but as a link. It allows less dependence on market shifts and helps AMD make adjustments with changes in AI architectures.

The CPU Angle Is Quietly Becoming Stronger

Perhaps the most salient takeaway from the Barclays discussion was the inversion of the notion that AI kills CPUs. There was a pointed discussion about an acceleration in CPU demand based on AI. Inferencing workloads, agent orchestration, storage, data preprocessing, and app logics—all necessary functions—have a general computing requirement.

Historically, AI would be viewed as a CPU-cannibalizing technology. That paradigm is being turned on its head because AI is expanding the server footprint. This trend heavily benefits AMD because they have a very strong EPYC lineup and have barely penetrated the enterprise market yet. The management pointed out that they have seen increasing traction within enterprise servers due to modernization cycles and AI adoption.

From an investment perspective, it represents a secondary growth trajectory that frequently goes unappreciated. It doesn’t end there. It goes beyond its AI advantage on GPUs. It also encompasses a structurally larger CPU market coexisting with AI adoption.

Why AMD’s Valuation Is De-Risking Fast

At $221, AMD appears rich on short-term sight, with an estimate for FY2025 EPS of 3.97, implying a forward multiple of 55.8x. However, it’s the earnings progress vendors have factored into next year that truly matters, and that’s EPS of 6.44, taking the multiple down to 34.4x without budging a single cent. Earnings de-risking, not hope, is what’s driving valuations.

A target price of $250 is effectively a re-rate to approximately 38.8x FY2026 EPS ($250/$6.44), which is below 3 year average of 41x, still fair for a scaling businesses with a $1 trillion data center TAM story and visible platform catalysts underpinned by the MI450 build-out, Helios RS D, OpenAI 6GW agreement, and Oracle's pending Helios supercomputer rollout featuring 50,000 GPUs.

What Actually Keeps Management Up at Night

When it came to risk questions, the responses were very telling. It had nothing to do with competition or demand destruction but had everything to do with implementation. The availability of advanced packaging, high-bandwidth memory ("HBM"), power, and facilities for data centers was constantly brought up.

This is a different risk profile compared with most tech cycles. These are not unsolved problems, but they do have a need for collaboration among suppliers, governments, and utilities. The management team expressed confidence in AMD's supply chain partnerships and cited encouraging progress on power infrastructure, specifically within the U.S. and sovereign AI nations.

It would appear that progress will be bottlenecked by logistics and implementation, as opposed to wanting to spend.

Bottom Line

While there is indeed an AI opportunity at AMD, its broader strength and value exist as a flexible and general-purpose computing platform. Executing on its strategy, it appears poised to leverage compounding growth across CPUs, GPUs, and systems with attractive margins.

data.title: Silver Surges, Far Outpacing Gold's Rally This Year
data.description:
The casual reader of financial news is probably aware that gold’s glitter is shining brightly this year on the heels of a 60%-plus year-to-date rally. But the sparkle looks dull next to silver’s rise.

Using a set of ETFs to track the two metals highlights that silver has surged nearly 119% this year, based on the iShares Silver Trust (SLV). The stellar increase is nearly twice the gain for gold, based on SPDR Gold Shares (GLD).

What’s driving silver’s upside explosion? A mix of factors, according to analysts, including stockpiling related to the growing demand for the metal in electronics, alternative energy applications, electric vehicles and artificial intelligence and data centers.

A report published this week by Oxford Economics and the Silver Institute, an industry group, outlined a bullish case for the metal’s industrial demand outlook:

"Silver is poised to play a pivotal 'next-generation metal' role across industries critical to the green energy transition and digital transformation over the coming decade," the report advised. “As digitalization and AI adoption accelerate, so too does the demand for critical materials involved in their applications—silver a critical one among them.” A press release for the report predicts: “Sectors such as solar energy (PV), automotive electric vehicles (EVs) and their infrastructure, and data centers and artificial intelligence (AI) will drive industrial demand higher through 2030.”

Adding to silver’s allure is the supply shortage that has prevailed in recent years. “We have a market that has been undersupplied for the past five years, and we still have regional stocks dislocation,” Suki Cooper, analyst at Standard Chartered, observes.

Monetary drivers are also said to be part of the bullish aura. Although silver plays second fiddle to gold as a perceived “safe haven” asset, it’s a facet that’s resonating lately for both metals. Adding to momentum on this front is the Federal Reserve’s third cut in interest rates this week, which lessens the opportunity cost for holding precious metals by reducing the attractiveness of fixed-income securities via lower yields.

Some analysts are speculating that silver could rally to a record $100 an ounce in the months and years ahead. Perhaps, but history reminds that silver, which is essentially an industrial metal, is no stranger to booms and busts, driven in part by the economic cycle. Reviewing silver’s price chart since 1990 reminds that spectacular rallies can be followed by equally epic declines.

The cycle is very much in the boom phase. Estimating the peak for silver is probably a function of when supply catches up with demand.

“While $100 silver is undoubtedly possible, commodity markets tend to rise to levels where cyclicality causes production to increase, inventories to build, and consumers to seek alternatives,” notes Andrew Hecht, a commodities trader and analyst.

Fundamentals will probably apply in the longer run, but short-term windows can be dominated by animal spirits, and so, caveat emptor applies.

“It is virtually impossible to pick tops in markets as aggressive rallies often defy technical levels and supply/demand fundamentals,” Hecht reminds.

For strategic-minded investors, there’s a case for routinely holding a mix of gold and silver as a risk management component. As I discussed earlier this year at TMC Research, “Silver’s role as a portfolio diversifier complements gold’s role as a hedging asset at times.” As a result, “Holding both metals to hedge some forms of tail risk for stocks and bonds, in other words, looks like a reasonable strategy for the long run, as opposed to betting that either metal alone will always outshine the other.”

Original Post
